query: What is the Vue reactivity system and how does it work internally?

competitors.label: Reactivity in Depth
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue deep dive on Proxy-based tracking, effect registration, trigger behavior, and common reactivity gotchas.
theirStrengths: The official guide is authoritative on track, trigger, effects, WeakMap storage, and runtime reactivity trade-offs.
theirGaps: It is broader and less compressed around the simplest production mental model for how Vue updates the UI.

realWorldUseCases: It allows your UI to automatically update when underlying data changes — without manually manipulating the DOM.
When data changes, Vue automatically re-runs or re-renders the parts of the app that depend on that data.
followUpConfusion: Vue wraps them in a proxy that intercepts reads and writes to track changes.
When a component renders and accesses reactive properties, Vue records which reactive values were used.

realWorldUseCases: The official guide already grounds reactivity in dependency tracking and automatic UI updates, so the conceptual baseline is strong.
actionableExamples: The docs include direct pseudo-code and lower-level examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is more compact on the exact internal loop interviewers probe first: proxy read, dependency map, trigger, rerender.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one smaller destructuring-loses-tracking example to make that gotcha visible at a glance.

competitors.winReason: The official guide is deeper on internals, but our page is stronger as a concise interview answer because it compresses Proxy tracking, dependency storage, and rerender behavior into one readable mental model.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because reactivity internals, refs, and update mechanics are core Vue interview themes.
theirStrengths: It is a strong interview-prep hub for Vue fundamentals and internals-adjacent questions.
theirGaps: The category page is broader than this exact internal reactivity walkthrough.

realWorldUseCases: Reactive objects trigger updates only for the affected properties.
Combined with virtual DOM diffing, this provides excellent runtime performance even for large applications.
followUpConfusion: These dependencies are stored in an internal data structure called a Dependency Map (a WeakMap under the hood).
Every time a reactive property is accessed during rendering, Vue calls track() to register the current watcher or computed effect as a dependency.

realWorldUseCases: The bank is clearly relevant for Vue reactivity questions, but it is less explicit about the exact internal dependency-map story.
actionableExamples: GreatFrontEnd remains competitive on interview-style worked examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less specific about track, trigger, WeakMap storage, and the bridge from reactivity to rerendering.

actionableExamples: Keep the internal explanation grounded in one reactive-object example so it does not turn into a framework-implementation essay.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a Vue interview hub, but our page is stronger because it explains the actual dependency-tracking loop instead of only naming reactivity as a concept.

competitors.label: Understanding Vue's Reactive Data
competitors.selectionReason: ThisDot has a topic-direct article on Vue reactive data and how Vue 3 tracks and responds to state changes.
theirStrengths: It is directly focused on Vue's reactive data model and user-facing update behavior.
theirGaps: It is lighter on the exact internal dependency map and trigger pipeline than our dedicated answer.

realWorldUseCases: It allows your UI to automatically update when underlying data changes — without manually manipulating the DOM.
This ensures that only the parts of the app that depend on the modified property are re-run, leading to efficient updates instead of full-page re-renders.
followUpConfusion: Vue records which reactive values were used.
When a reactive value changes, Vue triggers an update by looking up all the effects

realWorldUseCases: The tutorial is strong on the user-facing meaning of reactivity and automatic updates.
actionableExamples: It includes understandable reactive-data examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is stronger on the next-level interview follow-up around dependency tracking, WeakMap storage, and how computed and watch build on the same foundation.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one smaller computed-versus-watch example to show how they sit on top of the same reactivity core.

competitors.winReason: ThisDot is a solid reactivity explainer, but our page is stronger because it bridges the user-visible behavior to the actual internal dependency-tracking and rerender mechanics.
